faq.question: Is Seattle good for families?
faq.answer: Yes for outdoors and tech incomes — if you accept grey winters and housing costs.

faq.question: How much does a family typically need per month here?
faq.answer: Many families spend $7,500–$11,000/month all-in before private school.

faq.question: Is housing hard to find here?
faq.answer: Competitive on the Eastside (Bellevue, Redmond) — start searching 8–12 weeks ahead and have deposit funds and references ready before viewing. Properties in top school zones receive multiple applications within 48 hours.

faq.question: Do children need international school here, or can local schools work?
faq.answer: Strong public options in Lake Washington SD and Bellevue SD — school quality varies significantly by zone, so research catchment areas at your target district's website before choosing a neighborhood.

faq.question: Is healthcare easy to access as a newcomer?
faq.answer: Major hospital systems (UW Medicine, Swedish Health, Virginia Mason Franciscan) provide excellent care — all access is insurance-based. Confirm your employer plan's network before choosing coverage.

faq.question: Do you need a car in Seattle?
faq.answer: City pockets can use transit; Eastside suburban life usually needs a car.

faq.question: How difficult is the paperwork and bureaucracy after moving?
faq.answer: Washington DOL and school enrolment — straightforward but timeboxed.

faq.question: What usually surprises families after arrival?
faq.answer: How June–September redeem the rainy season — plan summer travel accordingly.

weather.disclaimer: These values are long-term monthly climatologies from NASA POWER (MERRA-2 reanalysis) for the nearest model grid cell to these coordinates — not a single city-centre weather station. Spatial resolution is about 50 km; coastal belts, hills, and dense urban cores can differ. Precipitation is corrected MERRA-2 rainfall; rainy-day counts are approximated from monthly totals.
familyHighlights: Warmest month on average: Jul (mean daily high ~30°C); coolest: Dec (mean daily low ~-4°C).
Most rainfall on average: Nov (~188 mm total); driest: Jul (~19 mm).
Winter nights can dip near freezing (Jan, Feb, Mar, Apr, Nov, Dec) — reliable home heating and warm layers for school commutes matter for children.
Very wet months mean waterproofs, covered waiting at school pickup, and extra room to dry uniforms and shoes.

tagline: Texas capital — fast growth, live music, and no state income tax
summary: Austin mixes tech hiring, warm weather, and Hill Country weekends. Trade-offs are traffic on I-35, summer heat, and a tight family rental market in top school zones.

actionChecklist.label: Check your visa status — Visa Waiver Program nationals use ESTA for short visits; working families normally need an employer-sponsored visa before arrival
Pick central Austin vs Westlake / Lake Travis / Round Rock before you search — school districts and commute times diverge fast
Map Eanes ISD, Lake Travis ISD, Leander ISD, and Austin ISD boundaries — ratings swing block by block

Apply for your Social Security Number (SSN — required for payroll, banking, and tax) at your nearest Social Security Administration office (ssa.gov). Bring your passport, visa, and I-94 arrival record (download at cbp.dhs.gov/i94).

Arrange US health insurance before your first day in Austin — either through your employer's group plan or a private IPMI (International Private Medical Insurance) policy. In the US, a single ER visit without insurance costs $2,000–$10,000.
Open a US bank account immediately — Frost is a common Texas choice alongside national banks

Schedule a Texas DPS (Department of Public Safety) appointment for your driver's licence at dps.texas.gov — appointment slots fill 4–6 weeks out, especially in summer. Austin is car-dependent; a Texas licence is required within 90 days of arriving.

Pack for Texas summer heat (38–42°C / 100–108°F from June–August) — confirm your apartment has functioning AC before signing, build an emergency supply kit ahead of the spring tornado season (March–May), and download the TexasAlerts emergency notification app.

bestFor: Tech and semiconductor families tied to Austin hiring waves
Outdoor households wanting lakes and Hill Country within an hour
Parents who value strong suburban publics when budgets allow
No-state-income-tax budgeting versus California peers
watchOutFor: I-35 and MoPac congestion punish the wrong commute pairing
Summer heat and UV index demand hydration plans for kids
Flash flooding in low-water crossings — never drive through running water
Property tax bills can surprise newcomers — verify homestead exemptions

residency.title: Registration & Social Security Number
residency.tip: Texas DPS (Department of Public Safety) online scheduling books weeks out — reserve early.
items: Apply for your SSN (Social Security Number) at your nearest SSA (Social Security Administration) office — bring your passport, visa stamp, and I-94 arrival record (download at cbp.dhs.gov/i94). Required for payroll, tax filing, and banking.
Update addresses with employer, bank, and SSA after every move.
Texas driver's licence within 90 days at DPS — bring two proofs of address.
Texas has no state income tax — you still owe federal tax; use a CPA the first year.
Confirm employer green-card sponsorship timelines if that matters to your family.

banking.title: Banking
banking.tip: Chase, Wells Fargo, Bank of America, and Frost Bank all serve relocations — bring passport and visa.
items: National banks (Chase, Wells Fargo, Bank of America) plus Frost Bank (a respected Texas-based regional bank with strong Austin presence) cover most everyday banking needs — branches throughout Central Austin and major suburbs.
Passport, visa, I-94, and local address open accounts — SSN helps but is not always day-one required.

Wise and Revolut bridge international currency transfers until your US payroll activates — both apps work from day one without a Social Security Number and are widely used in Austin's international tech community.
Landlords expect ACH, check, or Zelle — set up bill pay early.

housing.summary: Zilker and Travis Heights feel urban; Circle C and Steiner Ranch trade commute for yards; Bee Cave and Lakeway skew toward Lake Travis schools.
searchPortalsIntro: Zillow and Realtor.com cover most single-family rentals.
Apartments.com helps downtown and Domain towers.
Tip: verify floodplain maps before signing — creeks rise quickly.

searchPortals.label: Zillow.com — Austin and Hill Country inventory
Realtor.com — school overlays and leases
Apartments.com — mid-rise stock
Facebook groups — search: 'Austin families housing'

whatYouNeedToRent: Valid passport and US visa
Employment verification letter with salary confirmation
3 months of bank statements and last 3 payslips (or offer letter if newly arrived)
Most landlords require income of roughly 3× monthly rent
1–2 months security deposit
US bank account for ACH transfer or personal check

schools.summary: Westlake and Lake Travis publics draw families willing to pay housing premiums; Austin ISD has magnets but lottery pressure.
schools.publicSystem: Texas public schools are free; instruction is English with ESL support. Bond-funded facilities vary by district.
schools.internationalOptions: A handful of IB and bilingual tracks exist — fewer than Houston or DFW but improving with hiring.
schools.languageNotes: Spanish is widely spoken; Mandarin and Hindi tutoring clusters follow tech hiring.
schools.tip: If you need a specific high school, confirm attendance zones — Austin growth re-zones faster than maps update.

options.type: Eanes ISD and Lake Travis ISD
options.description: Suburban academics with strong parent involvement — housing costs reflect demand.
Competitive entry — track application windows and lottery dates at txcharterschools.org each January. IDEA Public Schools and KIPP Texas are the most established charter networks in the Austin metro.

Westlake Hills and Central Austin independent schools serve families who prefer a private environment with small class sizes — apply 12+ months ahead as the most sought-after campuses have significant waitlists.

childcare.summary: Centres cluster near tech campuses — infant rooms stay tight.
daycareItems: Texas public pre-K for qualifying four-year-olds — check TEA eligibility
Private centres often $1,200–$2,000/month for infants
DFPS licensing search before you pay deposits
Bilingual Spanish programmes are easy to find
nannyItems: Full-time nannies often $18–$28/hr gross depending on experience
Payroll services simplify federal and Texas unemployment filings
Start outreach 6–8 weeks before arrival in peak hiring seasons
whereToFindItems: Care.com and UrbanSitter
Search 'Austin parent groups' on Google
Corporate backup-care stipends from tech employers

healthcare.tip: Ascension Seton, St. David's, and Dell Children's anchor paediatrics — confirm in-network lists.
items: Employer PPOs dominate — read mental health and urgent-care tiers
Dell Children's handles complex paediatrics — referrals via paediatrician

Cedar fever (mountain cedar pollen) hits Austin hard each December–February — budget for allergy clinic visits and antihistamine supplies; the condition affects most newcomers during their first season.
Heat illness risk for outdoor sports — hydrate and schedule morning practices

Travel insurance or COBRA bridges coverage gaps between job roles — important in Austin's startup ecosystem where employer coverage ends on your exact last working day and individual plans take time to activate.

safety.summary: Suburbs are generally calm — research pockets east of I-35 and check flood history.
items: Property crime happens — lock cars and porches even in 'quiet' blocks

Hail storms are common in central Texas spring season — dent-resistant covered parking matters; check whether your lease includes covered parking and verify your car insurance includes comprehensive hail damage cover.
Wildfire smoke from distant burns occasionally degrades air quality

Austin's nightlife districts (6th Street, Rainey Street) are lively and generally safe — apply normal big-city awareness at night, use Uber or Lyft rather than flagging street taxis, and stay with a group after 11pm.

Austin's popular swimming holes (Barton Springs Pool, Blue Hole in Wimberley) require life jackets for children — currents are stronger than they appear and drowning incidents occur each year at unsupervised spots.

faq.question: Is Austin good for families?
faq.answer: Yes for tech salaries and outdoor life — heat and sprawl are the main lifestyle taxes.

faq.question: How much does a family typically need per month here?
faq.answer: Roughly $5,800–$8,500/month all-in for many suburban setups.

faq.question: Is housing hard to find here?
faq.answer: Tight in good school zones (Eanes ISD, Lake Travis ISD) — move decisively when you find the right listing. Good properties in top school zones receive multiple applications within 24 hours of being posted.

faq.question: Do children need international school here, or can local schools work?
faq.answer: Strong public districts exist — match address to district intentionally.

faq.question: Is healthcare easy to access as a newcomer?
faq.answer: Good hospital networks (St. David's HealthCare, Ascension Seton) with strong pediatric departments — employer insurance is the standard access route. Get coverage active before your first day in Austin.

faq.question: Do you need a car in Austin?
faq.answer: Almost always — transit does not replace suburban family life.

faq.question: How difficult is the paperwork and bureaucracy after moving?
faq.answer: Texas DMV and utilities — similar to other US sun-belt moves.

faq.question: What usually surprises families after arrival?
faq.answer: How fast growth changed commute patterns — test rush hour before choosing a suburb.

weather.disclaimer: These values are long-term monthly climatologies from NASA POWER (MERRA-2 reanalysis) for the nearest model grid cell to these coordinates — not a single city-centre weather station. familyHighlights: Warmest month on average: Aug (mean daily high ~41°C); coolest: Jan (mean daily low ~-4°C).
Most rainfall on average: May (~111 mm total); driest: Feb (~44 mm).
Mean daily highs reach about 32°C or more in Apr, May, Jun, Jul, Aug, Sep, Oct — plan air-conditioning, shade, and limited midday outdoor time for babies and young children.
Peak months can average above 35°C for daily highs — schedule playgrounds, walks, and errands for mornings or evenings when possible.
Winter nights can dip near freezing (Jan, Feb, Mar, Nov, Dec) — reliable home heating and warm layers for school commutes matter for children.

tagline: Global English-language hub — state + independent schools and a vast jobs market
summary: London mixes world-class museums, diverse boroughs, and the UK's largest jobs market. Families weigh strong schooling options against high rent, Tube commutes, and post-Brexit visa rules. Catchment and borough choice drive both lifestyle and school access.

actionChecklist.label: Confirm your UK visa route before travelling — a tourist visa does not grant the right to work or enroll children in state schools. Employment visa, Skilled Worker, or Global Talent routes are the most common for relocating families.

Start your housing search 8–12 weeks before your move date — London family flats in zones 1–3 (Islington, Richmond, Hampstead, Putney) move fast and require a deposit within 48 hours of viewing. Budget ~£3,500–£6,000/month for a 3-bedroom.

Apply to schools following your borough's admissions rules — state school places are allocated by borough councils based on address; most offer places in October for the following September. Independent school applications need 12–18 months lead time.
Register with a GP surgery after your address is fixed — NHS access flows through registration
Open a UK bank account (HSBC, Barclays, or Monzo) — bring your passport, visa, and proof of UK address. Monzo and Revolut open within days and work as a bridge while your main account processes.

Apply for your National Insurance number (NI number — the UK's tax and social security identifier, required for payroll) at gov.uk/apply-national-insurance-number. Bring proof of identity and your right-to-work documents.

Research nursery and primary school places simultaneously — in London, demand for both nurseries (for ages 2–4) and state primary schools in popular boroughs (Islington, Richmond, Wandsworth) is intense. Apply for your nursery place as soon as you have a London address, and check state primary school admissions at your borough council's website.

Register at your nearest NHS GP (General Practitioner — the UK's free family doctor) as soon as you have a London address — NHS registration is free and available to all legal residents regardless of visa type. Bring your visa, passport, and proof of address. GP registration is the gateway to all other NHS services.

bestFor: Families wanting English-medium life with global employers and strong transport
Parents who value free state schools (ages 5–18) when catchments work out
Culture-heavy weekends — museums, theatre, and parks without leaving the city
Households comfortable with flat living and Tube-or-bus school runs
watchOutFor: Rent is very high — catchment research must happen alongside budget
Visa and NHS surcharge rules change — confirm before long school commitments
International school places are tight — apply many months ahead
Grey winters and smaller homes versus US sprawl — set expectations early

visa.summary: The UK is outside the EU. Most visitors enter on a Standard Visitor visa or visa-free rules depending on nationality — confirm before travel. Long-term work usually requires a Skilled Worker visa with employer sponsorship; there is no generic remote-work visa comparable to EU digital nomad routes.
visa.tip: Search 'UK skilled worker visa gov.uk' on Google for current salary thresholds and document lists — rules change with government budgets.

options.detailTitle: Standard Visitor — short family visits and scouting
options.duration: Usually up to 6 months per visit — confirm for your nationality
options.description: For tourism and scouting schools — not for living and working long-term.
details: Many nationalities visit visa-free for short trips; others apply for a Standard Visitor visa before travel.
No right to work in the UK — including most remote work tied to a UK entity.
Use a scouting trip to view neighbourhoods and schools, then apply for the correct work or family visa from outside the UK if you plan to stay.
Search 'UK Standard Visitor visa gov.uk' on Google for the checklist that matches your passport.

officialLink.label: UK Standard Visitor visa official gov.uk

options.detailTitle: Skilled Worker visa — main employed route
options.description: Requires a licensed UK employer and a job offer that meets skill and salary tests.
details: Your employer issues a Certificate of Sponsorship — you then apply online and usually provide biometrics.
Dependants normally apply as linked family members — confirm school access and NHS surcharge payment rules before you move.
Healthcare: most applicants pay the Immigration Health Surcharge for NHS access — treat official guidance as the source of truth.
Search 'UK Skilled Worker visa gov.uk' on Google for salary thresholds and eligible occupations.

officialLink.label: UK Skilled Worker visa gov.uk

residency.title: Registration & National Insurance
residency.tip: Council tax and school admissions tie to your borough address — secure proof early.
items: Register children for schools through your local council process — rules differ by borough.
National Insurance (NI — UK social security number for tax and benefits) is arranged through employer or official channels.
Update your address with banks, HMRC, and your GP when you move.
Driving licences swap from many countries within DVLA rules — search 'exchange foreign licence DVLA' on Google.

banking.title: Banking
banking.tip: High-street banks may need a face appointment — digital banks can bridge.
items: Barclays, HSBC, Lloyds, and NatWest are common for newcomers with address proof.

Wise and Revolut open within 24 hours of signing up and work as a bridge until your full current account clears — both are widely used by London expats to cover the first 2–4 weeks before their main account is active.

Rent in London typically requires a UK sort code and account number for direct debit — confirm with your landlord whether Monzo or Revolut is accepted, as some property management companies require a traditional bank account.

HSBC and Barclays are the two most expat-friendly UK banks for international families — HSBC in particular offers a pre-arrival international account switch service for existing HSBC customers moving from abroad.

housing.summary: Family demand concentrates in zones 2–4 and southwest corridors — expect competition and agent fees in some segments.
searchPortalsIntro: Rightmove and Zoopla dominate long-term lets.
Search by borough and Tube line — commute time is decisive.
Tip: short-let a month while you view in person.

Zoopla.co.uk — strong London inventory
Facebook groups — search: 'London family housing'

whatYouNeedToRent: Passport and visa or BRP (Biometric Residence Permit — UK ID card for many visa holders)
Proof of income or employer letter
References — previous landlord and employer
Deposit capped under tenant-fee rules — confirm current statute

schools.summary: London mixes outstanding state schools (when catchments align), large academies, and a competitive independent sector. Borough admissions rules differ — read your council's site before you rent.
schools.publicSystem: State schools are free; instruction is English. Reception starts around age 4; secondary transfer at 11+ varies by borough. Faith and grammar streams add complexity — research early.
schools.internationalOptions: IB, American, and French lycée-style schools cluster in west and south-west London plus Surrey borders. Fees vary widely — apply 12+ months ahead for popular leavers' destinations.
schools.languageNotes: English dominates; community languages thrive in many boroughs. Extra tutoring is easy to find but adds cost.
schools.tip: Let school admissions rules guide your borough shortlist — guessing catchments from maps alone is risky.

options.description: Free; quality varies sharply by borough and Ofsted band — visit schools and read latest reports.
Selective, often centuries-old institutions — long waiting lists for entry years.
Serve mobile families — check leavers' destinations and coach networks.

childcare.summary: Nurseries (private and some council-funded) and registered childminders cover under-fives — demand is high in inner boroughs.
daycareItems: Full-time nursery places often cost ~$1,800–$2,800/month in central zones before government schemes
15 or 30 funded hours may apply for ages 3–4 — check gov.uk and your borough portal
Waiting lists are normal — join several nurseries as soon as you have a move date
nannyItems: Live-out nannies often ~$16–$22/hr gross — employer NI and payroll rules apply
Au pairs suit some households but have hour and accommodation rules — verify Home Office guidance
Agencies cluster in west London — compare placement fees carefully
whereToFindItems: Search 'London nanny agency OFSTED' on Google
Local parent Facebook groups by borough
Childcare.co.uk listings

healthcare.tip: Register everyone at a GP surgery soon after your address is live — specialists route through GPs.
items: NHS care is residency and visa dependent — many visa routes pay the Immigration Health Surcharge for access
Private BUPA / AXA-style cover is common for faster paediatric access
Major paediatric hospitals include Great Ormond Street and Evelina London — emergencies use A&E by postcode
Pharmacies handle minor illnesses; NHS 111 lines help overnight
Dental NHS slots are tight — private dentists are easier short term

safety.summary: London is broadly safe for families who pick neighbourhoods thoughtfully — transport crime and scooter theft matter more than violent crime in most family postcodes.
items: Knife crime headlines rarely reflect daily life in typical family wards — still read police.uk maps
Phone snatching on bikes is a real annoyance — don't flash devices at Tube doors
Road safety beats crime for young kids — busy roundabouts need training
Terrorism awareness is part of the national posture — follow police advice during alerts
Flooding and heat pockets vary — check basement flats before signing

faq.question: Is London good for families?
faq.answer: Yes when borough, commute, and school strategy align — costs are high but services are broad.

faq.question: How much does a family typically need per month here?
faq.answer: Roughly $7,500–$11,000/month all-in before private school fees.

faq.question: Is housing hard to find here?
faq.answer: Very competitive in zones 1–3, particularly in south and west London family areas (Putney, Richmond, Islington) — proceed fast when you find the right property and prepare references and deposit in advance.

faq.question: Do children need international school here, or can local schools work?
faq.answer: State schools can work well; international schools cluster for specific curricula — see Schools.

faq.question: Is healthcare easy to access as a newcomer?
faq.answer: Register with a GP — NHS access depends on visa and surcharge payment rules.

faq.question: Do you need a car in London?
faq.answer: Often unnecessary within Tube zones 1–3 for daily errands — but most families with children in the outer zones (Richmond, Wimbledon, Chiswick) find a car genuinely useful for weekend activities and school events.

faq.question: How difficult is the paperwork and bureaucracy after moving?
faq.answer: Council tax registration, National Insurance setup, and school admissions forms are the main post-arrival tasks — moderately involved but manageable if you tackle them in the first 2 weeks after getting a confirmed address.

faq.question: What usually surprises families after arrival?
faq.answer: How much space budget buys versus other capitals — adjust expectations early.

tagline: English-speaking EU hub — tech employers and coastal suburbs
summary: Dublin is Ireland's capital and main expat gateway — English-speaking, EU membership, and a strong tech payroll. Families weigh rain, housing scarcity, and car-light city living against village-style suburbs on the DART line.

actionChecklist.label: Non-EU applicants: line up an employment permit or other immigration permission before arrival — employers sponsor most tech routes

Start your housing search 3–4 months before your move date — Dublin's southside family suburbs (Blackrock, Dalkey, Rathfarnham) move very fast and competition for 3+ bedroom rentals is intense. Budget ~€2,500–€4,000/month for a family home.
Apply to private or Educate Together schools 12–18 months before your move — the best international-friendly schools in South Dublin fill places early and cannot be bypassed with a late application.
Apply for a PPS number as soon as you have an address — needed for tax, payroll, and many services
Register with a GP and decide on private health cover — public access exists but queues vary

Open an Irish bank account (Bank of Ireland or AIB) once you have your PPS number and proof of address. Alternatively, use Revolut or N26 as a bridge account from day one — both are available without an Irish address.

Research crèche (nursery — Irish term for daycare for children under 3) options near your neighborhood before arriving — Dublin crèches in Blackrock, Rathmines, and Clontarf have 6–12 month waiting lists. Expect to pay ~€1,200–€1,800/month full-time. The National Childcare Subsidy (NCS) reduces costs once you have a PPS number.

Dublin is a safe city for families in the residential suburbs — petty theft and occasional antisocial behavior in the city center late at night are the main concerns. The south Dublin suburbs (Blackrock, Dalkey, Ranelagh) are among the safest family areas. Be aware that healthcare wait times in public hospitals are long — private insurance is strongly recommended.

bestFor: Tech and pharma families on employer-sponsored permits
Parents wanting English-speaking EU life with US multinationals nearby
Households happy with coastal walks and village-style suburbs on the DART
Families prioritising IB or British-curriculum schools
watchOutFor: Rental supply is tight — expect bidding and references
Rain and short winter days — plan indoor routines
Commutes from Wicklow or Meath add time — test school runs
Immigration paperwork rewards patience — keep printouts of every permit

residency.title: Registration & PPS number
residency.tip: PPS applications need evidence of why you need the number — employment letters and leases help.
items: Apply for a Personal Public Service (PPS) number via MyWelfare.ie or designated centres — you need a reason such as work or tax.
EU/EEA nationals: register residency if required; non-EU nationals: keep passport stamps and IRP (Irish Residence Permit) appointments current.

Register with Revenue (Ireland's tax authority) for your tax credits and standard rate band as soon as payroll starts — failure to claim your credits means you pay emergency tax (40%) from day one. Register at myaccount.revenue.ie.
Update your address on all permits when you move — landlords often require RTB registration awareness.

banking.title: Banking
banking.tip: AIB, Bank of Ireland, and Permanent TSB are common retail choices; Revolut Ireland works for day-one spending.
items: Passport, proof of Irish address, and PPS unlock most current accounts.
Mortgage and credit history from abroad rarely transfers — plan for cash deposits on rentals.
SEPA IBAN (your Irish account number in the international standard format) is required for payroll direct deposit and school fee standing orders — confirm your bank provides this at account opening.

Wise and Revolut bridge incoming international transfers while your Irish current account is being processed — typically takes 1–2 weeks for new customers and is the standard gap-filler for newly arrived Dublin expats.

visa.summary: EU/EEA citizens can live in Ireland without a visa. Non-EU families usually need employment permits and a residence permission from INIS — rules are national, not Dublin-specific.
visa.tip: Keep copies of every employment permit, visa, and IRP card — schools and banks ask repeatedly.

options.detailTitle: EU / EEA citizens — arriving in Ireland
options.description: EU/EEA nationals can live in Ireland without a visa. Register for a PPS number when you need tax or payroll access, and follow any residency registration rules that apply to your situation.
details: Bring a valid passport or national ID.
Apply for a PPS number through MyWelfare.ie when you have work or tax reasons.
After five years of legal residence you may qualify for permanent residence — confirm with Irish Naturalisation and Immigration Service (INIS) guidance.
Cross-border commuters should track social insurance and tax treaties if working remotely for non-Irish employers.

options.detailTitle: Employment permits — employer-sponsored route
options.description: Most non-EU professionals arrive on a Critical Skills Employment Permit or General Employment Permit sponsored by an Irish employer, then secure an Irish Residence Permit (IRP) stamp aligned with that work.
details: Employer files with the Department of Enterprise, Trade and Employment for the permit — processing times vary by route.
Critical Skills permits target shortage occupations and can offer a faster path to longer stamps — verify current salary thresholds.
After approval, book immigration registration to receive your IRP card — children need their own permissions documented.
Dependents may have work rights depending on the primary permit — read INIS notices for the year you land.

officialLink.label: Irish employment permits official — enterprise.gov.ie

options.detailTitle: Short stay visit — scouting Dublin
options.description: Valid for a scouting trip. No right to work, no extensions.
details: Ireland is not in the Schengen Area — visitor rules are Irish and separate from continental stamps.
Use short visits to view southside suburbs and school corridors.
Working without permission is not allowed — line up permits before children start school.
Search 'INIS immigration Ireland' on Google for current visitor and permit pages.

housing.summary: Southside suburbs (Blackrock, Dun Laoghaire, Ballsbridge, Rathgar) dominate family demand — start 3–4 months early and budget agent fees.
searchPortalsIntro: Daft.ie is the default long-term portal — refresh alerts daily.
Rent.ie and Facebook housing groups supplement agency stock.
Tip: corporate relocations often use licensed letting agents — references and employer letters help.

searchPortals.label: Daft.ie — Ireland's largest property portal
MyHome.ie — major Irish rental portal
Rent.ie — landlord-direct listings
Facebook groups — search: 'Housing Dublin Expats' or 'Dublin Rentals' (direct landlord and community deals)

whatYouNeedToRent: Valid passport and immigration permission
PPS number — agencies and many landlords expect it
Employer reference and recent payslips
Deposit plus first month's rent — two months' deposit is common
Irish bank account for standing orders

schools.summary: English-medium national schools teach Irish as a subject; fee-paying secondaries and IB schools sit mostly south of the Liffey — apply early.
schools.publicSystem: Free national schools are English-medium with Irish lessons; Gaelscoileanna teach through Irish — match the pathway to your child's age and language comfort.
schools.internationalOptions: IB and British-curriculum schools cluster near Ballsbridge, Leopardstown, and south Dublin — fees often €15,000–€25,000/year with waiting lists.
schools.languageNotes: Daily life is English; learning some Irish helps socially in local schools.
schools.tip: Buy or rent inside the feeder pattern you want before Transition Year conversations begin.

options.description: Serve tech and diplomatic families — interviews and deposits are competitive.
Strong value if you secure an address in the right catchment.
Some families commute weekly from Dublin to rural secondaries — niche but exists.

childcare.summary: Creches are regulated by Tusla — subsidies through the National Childcare Scheme reduce bills for eligible families.
daycareItems: Full-day creche often €1,200–€1,800/month before subsidies
ECCE provides a free preschool year — check age windows
Apply for NCS subsidies as soon as PPS numbers exist for parents and children
Waitlists spike near Sandyford and Docklands offices
nannyItems: Registered childminders can be cheaper than city creches
Au pair programmes have strict immigration rules — verify with INIS
Payroll obligations apply if you hire full-time domestic staff
whereToFindItems: ChildcareFinder and local creche chains
Search 'Dublin parent networks' on Google
Workplace nursery partnerships at large employers

healthcare.tip: Register with a local GP immediately — specialists usually require referrals.
items: Public care exists but emergency waits vary — many expats layer private insurance (Vhi, Laya, Irish Life)
Prescription drugs are subsidised once you are in the system

Dental and optical care is almost entirely private in Ireland — budget ~€80–€150 per dental checkup and €100–€200 for an optical exam. Some employers include dental cover in their benefits packages; check before signing.
EHIC covers short EU visits but not routine Irish care for new arrivals
Mental health services need early booking — ask HR for employee assistance programmes

safety.summary: Dublin is generally safe — petty theft and antisocial behaviour rise late night near Temple Bar.
items: Use normal urban awareness on the Luas Green Line and city center late at night — Dublin is generally safe but the Luas stops near Jervis and O'Connell Street occasionally see antisocial behavior after midnight.

Bicycle theft is common throughout Dublin — always lock your frame to a fixed object in a well-lit area with a quality D-lock, and consider registering your bike at mybike.ie for added theft protection.
Coastal cliffs and piers need supervision with small children
Low-lying quays near the River Liffey can flood during heavy winter storms — check OPW (Office of Public Works) flood alerts at floodinfo.ie if your home is near the river or in a low-lying suburb.
Roads are left-hand — practise school-run junctions before winter dark

visa.label: Irish Naturalisation and Immigration Service (INIS) official

communityLinks.label: Search 'Dublin Expats' on Google — tips from residents and families

faq.question: Is Dublin good for families?
faq.answer: Yes for English-speaking EU access, tech salaries, and coastal suburbs — if you accept high rent and weather.

faq.question: How much does a family typically need per month here?
faq.answer: Many families land around $6,500–$9,500/month all-in; southside houses push higher.

faq.question: Is housing hard to find here?
faq.answer: Very — start three to four months early, polish references, and expect competition on every viewing.

faq.question: Do children need international school here, or can local schools work?
faq.answer: English-medium national schools are common; international schools add predictability for mobile families — see Schools.

faq.question: Is healthcare easy to access as a newcomer?
faq.answer: Register with a GP first; public queues vary so many households add private insurance for faster access.

faq.question: Do you need a car in Dublin?
faq.answer: City centre families often mix DART, bus, and bike; suburban school runs usually need at least one car.

faq.question: How difficult is the paperwork and bureaucracy after moving?
faq.answer: Moderate Irish stack: PPS number, IRP appointments for non-EU, bank proof-of-address loops — allow a few weeks.

faq.question: What usually surprises families after arrival?
faq.answer: How long letting agents take to reply — persistence and local phone numbers help.
